titlestmt.titleproper: Cornell Theory Center Visualization Group files53-16-3705
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Julia Parker

publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2022-09-07 18:46:36 UTC.

change.item: This resource was modified by the ArchivesSpace Preprocessor developed by the Harvard Library (https://github.com/harvard-library/archivesspace-preprocessor)

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.unittitle: Cornell Theory Center Visualization Group files
did.abstract: The collection contains digital files, created by Chris Pelkie, of videotapes and DVDs from the Cornell Theory Center Visualization Group.

dao.xlink:title: Tape dumps 1 CRP

daodesc.p: Tape dumps 1 CRP

dao.xlink:title: CTC V12 Archive 2 HD

daodesc.p: CTC V12 Archive 2 HD

dao.xlink:title: CTC V12 Archive 3 HD

daodesc.p: CTC V12 Archive 3 HD

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu

bioghist.head: ORGANIZATIONAL HISTORY
bioghist.p: Clients of the Visualization Group were researchers (faculty, grad students, some undergrads, some corporate) of the Cornell Theory Center or CNSF (Cornell National Supercomputer Facility). Copyright notices, when present, usually attribute copyright to Cornell University. Copies were almost certainly given to the principal investigators in every case; other copies may have been distributed to other interested parties. The group ceased operations in 2008.
Chris Pelkie was the Scientific Visualization Producer for the group.

scopecontent.head: COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
scopecontent.p: The collection contains digital files, created by Chris Pelkie, of videotapes and DVDs from the Cornell Theory Center Visualization Group. The earliest tape is probably from 1988, and the most recent visualization movies (not necessarily tape) are from 2008. For the most part, these are scientific visualizations, i.e., graphics produced from numeric data. Most represent either/or time series or motion sequence (rotation, for example) of the visualized phenomena. There are a few live action videos, shot of the visualization studio or at trade shows or workshops, etc. Some of the more highly produced videos include narration and/or music beds. Many are silent. Most have titles/credits/attributions.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Cornell Theory Center Visualization Group files, #53-16-3705. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

did.unittitle: MPI Coll Comm II Steve Lantz
Mansfield, Talking Head etc., Manhattan Lecture
NIH Symposium Day 2 - 2: John Straub (Boston U)
Tom Coleman: Intro to Discrete Hedging 020321
Gerd Heber: Over the Shoulder Shot to Laptop
John Zollweg Partner Training 1: MPI Lectures 1
John Zollweg Partner Training 2: MPI Lectures 2, Parallel Programming
John Zollweg Partner Training 3: MPI Lectures, Benchmark
Lifka Partner Training 1: Intro to HPC at CTC, Module 1, HPC Cluster Computing
Lifka Partner Training 2: Module 1b, Killer APPS; Module 3, Research Management + Scheduling
Lifka Partner Training 3: Module - HW Design
Partner Training Will Law: end of lecture: Building a Windows HPC
Partner Training Will Law 2: Core Components of a Windows HPC Cluster
Lucy Walle: Tips on Blog HPC Cluster
Jarek Pillardy: Computational Biology on HPC
